nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving, heavily vegetated streams, peat swamps, and shallow forest pools with soft, acidic, tannin-stained blackwater; substrate of leaf litter, fine sand, and decomposing organic matter

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java moss, Bucephalandra, Anubias nana petite, floating plants)
driftwood and leaf litter for tannin release and shelter

diet.notes: Micropredator in the wild, feeding on tiny invertebrates, zooplankton, and small insects. In the aquarium, accepts high-quality micro-pellets, crushed flakes, and frozen/live micro-foods including baby brine shrimp, micro worms, daphnia, and cyclops. Mouth is very small — foods must be appropriately sized. Feed twice daily in small amounts.

goodWith: other Boraras species (with care — may hybridise)
small Corydoras (e.g., C. habrosus, C. pygmaeus)
other nano fish of similar size and temperament
avoidWith: any fish large enough to swallow them (most fish over 4–5 cm)
boisterous active fish that will outcompete them for food

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo gallery for Boraras naevus (species ID 68753). Verify current photo availability and license before use.

summary: A tiny (≤2 cm) Thailand-endemic nano fish described in 2009. Strawberry-orange body with a single bold black spot on the flank — no mid-lateral stripe — distinguishes it from related Boraras species. Shrimp-safe, peaceful, and well-suited to densely planted blackwater nano aquaria.
careNotes:
The Strawberry Rasbora (Boraras naevus) is one of the smallest aquarium fish available, reaching only 1.5–2 cm at maturity. It was formally described by Kottelat in 2009 and is endemic to Thailand. Its common name references the warm strawberry-orange body coloration; the single dark spot on the flank — lacking the mid-lateral stripe seen in B. merah (Phoenix Rasbora) and B. brigittae (Chili Rasbora) — is the key visual identification feature, though the three species are frequently confused in the trade.

A species-appropriate nano tank of at least 30 L is recommended. Soft, acidic water (pH 5.0–7.0, gH 1–8) is strongly preferred and replicates the peat-swamp blackwater habitat of the Malay Peninsula and Thailand. The addition of Indian almond leaves or alder cones to release tannins, a dark substrate, and dense planting with low flow will encourage natural behaviour and bring out the best coloration. Lighting should be subdued.

Keep in groups of at least 8 — larger groups of 12–20+ produce more confident shoaling behaviour and reduce individual stress. The Strawberry Rasbora is completely safe with dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina) and makes an excellent inhabitant for a shrimp-focused planted tank. Tankmates must be similarly tiny and non-aggressive; most fish larger than 4–5 cm may view them as prey.

Feeding requires appropriately scaled micro-foods. High-quality micro-pellets, crushed nano flakes, baby brine shrimp, micro worms, and daphnia are all accepted. Feed small amounts twice daily and ensure the fish are actually consuming food, as they can be outcompeted by faster tankmates.

See also: [phoenix-rasbora] (B. merah, fw-fish-212) — nearly identical care, distinguished by continuous mid-lateral stripe; [chili-rasbora] (B. brigittae) — darker red with black stripe and spot markings.
breedingNotes: Boraras naevus is an egg-scatterer that will spawn opportunistically in a mature, well-planted aquarium. Eggs and fry receive no parental care, and adults may eat eggs; dense fine-leaved plants (Java moss, spawning mops) improve survival. A dedicated breeding tank with a sponge-filtered 10–20 L setup, very soft acidic water (pH 5.5–6.5), and live micro-foods for conditioning is recommended for intentional breeding. Fry are extremely tiny on hatching and require infusoria or commercially prepared fry foods for the first week before advancing to baby brine shrimp nauplii. Breeding difficulty is moderate due to fry size.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Boraras naevus
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source. Provided adult size (2 cm), water parameters (pH 5.0–7.0, temperature 22–28°C, very soft water), native range (Thailand), habitat (slow-moving streams and swamps), diet, tankmate recommendations, and breeding notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Boraras naevus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cyprinidae, Cypriniformes), adult size, native range (Thailand), original description reference (Kottelat 2009). Confirmed species is Thailand endemic.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Chili Rasbora Care Guide
additional.notes: Used for comparative Boraras genus care guidance. Confirmed soft-acidic water requirements, nano tank sizing, micro-food diet, and shrimp compatibility applicable across the Boraras genus including B. naevus.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Boraras species overview
additional.notes: Provided identification notes distinguishing B. naevus (single spot, no mid-stripe) from B. merah (continuous stripe) and B. brigittae (stripe + spot). Confirmed 2009 Kottelat description and Thailand endemism.

additional.name: Kottelat, M. (2009) — Description of Boraras naevus, original species description
additional.notes: Original species description reference. Kottelat 2009, Ichthyol. Explor. Freshwat. 20(1):13–20. Confirmed taxonomic status, type locality (Thailand), and diagnosis relative to congeners.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer with no parental care. Will spawn in a mature planted tank but fry are extremely small and require infusoria initially. Best results with a dedicated breeding setup, very soft acidic water, and live foods for conditioning.
